coredata.dc:title: Novel electrochemical sensor from magnetic carbon dots and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide for sensitive measurement of tetrabromobisphenol A in beverages

Li, Shuangying
Tong, Yayan
Li, Zhi

Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Present work depicted a novel electrochemical sensor fabricated with magnetic carbon dots (M-CDs) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for selective measurement of 3,3′,5,5′-tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) in beverages. The M-CDs composite material revealed good electrocatalytic activity, and CTAB has strong hydrophobic interaction which enable it have good enrichment capacity of hydrophobic compounds, and combination of them further enhances the electrochemical signal. Hence CTAB decoration can markedly improve the detection performance of TBBPA. Electrochemical properties of the fabricated sensor was investigated through performing cyclic voltammetry (CV). The morphology and functional groups of the modified materials were examined with transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The results indicated that the synthesized material had a spherical-like structure, good dispersion properties and plenty of functional groups on the surface. The effects of incubation potential, incubation time, pH of electrolyte, and scanning rate on oxidation peak current were investigated. Under optimal conditions, the designed sensor had good linear range of 1 nM–1000 nM, and the detection of limit of the constructed sensor was 0.75 nM. The constructed sensor was utilized to detect TBBPA in vitamin water, scream drink and genki forest, and satisfactory detection performance had been achieved.

head.ce:title: Novel electrochemical sensor from magnetic carbon dots and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide for sensitive measurement of tetrabromobisphenol A in beverages

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Present work depicted a novel electrochemical sensor fabricated with magnetic carbon dots (M-CDs) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for selective measurement of 3,3′,5,5′-tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) in beverages. The M-CDs composite material revealed good electrocatalytic activity, and CTAB has strong hydrophobic interaction which enable it have good enrichment capacity of hydrophobic compounds, and combination of them further enhances the electrochemical signal. Hence CTAB decoration can markedly improve the detection performance of TBBPA. Electrochemical properties of the fabricated sensor was investigated through performing cyclic voltammetry (CV). The morphology and functional groups of the modified materials were examined with transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The results indicated that the synthesized material had a spherical-like structure, good dispersion properties and plenty of functional groups on the surface. The effects of incubation potential, incubation time, pH of electrolyte, and scanning rate on oxidation peak current were investigated. Under optimal conditions, the designed sensor had good linear range of 1 nM–1000 nM, and the detection of limit of the constructed sensor was 0.75 nM. The constructed sensor was utilized to detect TBBPA in vitamin water, scream drink and genki forest, and satisfactory detection performance had been achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: 3,3′,5,5′-Tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) is a commonly used brominated flame retardant in electronic products, plastic products and textiles (Yuan et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2021). However, a large amount of TBBPA has been released into the environment due to its extensive use, improper handling and discharge from the industries (Xiang et al., 2021). TBBPA has been detected in multiple environmental media (Fu et al., 2020), in addition, it also exists in animals and humans (Xiang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). It has been reported that TBBPA is a potential persistent organic pollutant with bioaccumulation and high toxicity. TBBPA can be bioaccumulated through the food chain to pose a threat to the human body (Wluka et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020). Studies have shown that TBBPA also has endocrine disrupting effects, and long-term exposure to TBBPA can cause endocrine disorders in humans (Yuan et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a). These potential hazards make it extremely urgent to develop sensitive and rapid technologies for quick detection of TBBPA.

Researchers have made tremendous efforts to develop reliable detection methods for determining TBBPA at trace levels. Nowadays, many methods have exhibited distinct merits for analysis of TBBPA such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Feng et al., 2019), ultra-high performance liquid chromatography equiped with triple quadrupole tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) (Yang et al., 2021), high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector (HPLC-DAD) (Liu et al., 2017a), high-performance liquid chromatography equiped with inductively coupled plasma tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-ICP-MS/MS) (Liu et al., 2017b), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Zhu et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2007; Barrett et al., 2015), Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Wang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020). These methods based on chromatographical technologies are highly sensitive and can detect multiple substances at the same time. However, high instrument maintenance costs, complicated operating skills and a large amount of sample consumption limit their widespread use. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a new detection technology to detect trace amount of TBBPA. Sensor earns good signal response and high selectivity, which is a good selection for detecting TBBPA, specially, electrochemical sensor. Electrochemical sensors have attracted many researchers's attention because of their high efficiency, rapidity, sensitivity and low cost (Zhao et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2022). At present, some studies have been reported. Shen et al. electropolymerized dopamine onto glassy carbon electrode to prepare an imprinted film, which was used as modified material to fabricate a sensor for sensitive and specific detection of TBBPA (Shen et al., 2018). Zhang et al. modified the composite of reduced graphene oxide, silver nanodendrites and molecular imprinting film onto electrode, which achived the determination of TBBPA in plastic products (Zhang et al., 2020). Zhang et al. designed and constructed a composite electrode by modifying carbon electrode with graphene/carbon nanotubes three-dimensional nanocomposites, and pyrrole was used to fabricate imprinted film by electrodeposition on to the composite electrode for high selectively determining TBBPA (Zhang et al., 2015). Hence, modification of the working electrode with new nanomaterial is a very critical way for developing electrochemical sensor to achieve sensitive and selective determination of TBBPA.

Recently, Carbon dots achieved enough attention due to their merits of low toxicity, biocompatibility, easy synthesis and good conductivity. As a new type of fluorescent material, CDs was firstly digged out accidently in the period of the electrophoretic purification of multi-walled carbon nanotubes derived from arc discharge soot (Xu et al., 2004). CDs have shown great potential in many aspects including bioimaging (Li et al., 2021b), biosensing (Hu et al., 2021), photocatalysis (Liang et al., 2022), electrocatalysis (Pei et al., 2021), and chemical sensing (Hou et al., 2016). The good conductivity of CDs make it possible to achieve application in electrochemical sensing. It has been reported that CDs have been used for simple and environment-friendly detection of 2,4-dichlorophenol by preparing CDs-CTAB and chitosan modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using electrostatic self-assembly technology and infrared assisted coating (Yu et al., 2016). Ghiasi et al. utilized graphene quantum dots, chitosan and niclel molybdate to decorate activated GCE, and the prepared nanocomposites modified electrochemical sensor exhibited good sensitivity for diazinon (Ghiasi et al., 2021). Polyaniline and carbon dots coated GCE was also reported to electrochemically detect 17β-estradiol with enhanced stability (Supchicksoonthorn et al., 2021).

At present, in terms of sensors, the main application of CDs is focused on optical sensors constructed based on their special fluorescene properties. However, there is still a small amount of research on CDs in electrochemical field. In contrast, the reports on CDs in electrochemical field were less than that in optical sensors. Due to the excellent properties, it has a wide exploration potential. Meanwhile, some metal oxides can markedly improve the electrochemical signal and further increase the sensitivity of the developed electrochemical sensors. Among the transition metal oxides, the ferrite family, especially Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs), stands out for its good chemical stability, easy preparation, high specific surface area, large adsorption capacity, good biocompatibility and low toxicity (Aquino et al., 2020; Vinodha et al., 2019; Venkataprasada et al., 2019). However, due to the low conductivity and easy agglomeration of Fe3O4 NPs, they have low dispersibility and catalytic activity, which limits their wide application in the domain of electrochemical analysis (Abbasa et al., 2019). This situation can be improved by combining Fe3O4 NPs with carbonaceous materials. This combination can greatly improve its electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity. El-Desoky et al. combined Fe3O4 with graphene, and found that its active surface area was significantly higher than that of Fe3O4 individually and the current response was significantly enhanced (El-Desoky et al., 2020). However, the high synthesis cost and complexity of carbonaceous materials, for example, graphene or carbon nanotubes, increased overall production cost for constructing the sensors. Therefore, considering the unique advantages of facile preparation of CDs, it will be a wonderful direction to design and fabricate novel, simple, sensitive and high selective electrochemical sensor using CDs in combination with Fe3O4 NPs.

In present work, maleic acid was employed as a carbon source to synthesize carbon dots and magnetic carbon dots were further synthesized with Fe3O4 NPs and as-prepared CDs by amide reaction. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) is a common cationic surfactant, which is composed of positively charged hydrophilic groups and negatively charged hydrophobic groups. Its long alkyl chain has a strong hydrophobic interaction with the phenols (Li et al., 2013a). For comprehensive consideration, CTAB was selected as the electrode surface comodifier. Upon the fact that TBBPA has been a significant pollutant, a sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor for TBBPA was designed and constructed using magnetic carbon dots and CTAB modified GCE. Differential pulse coltammetry (DPV) and CV were used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of electrode modified materials, and the specific groups and morphology of the fabricated materials were examined by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR).

N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was achieved from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). K3 [Fe(CN)6] was gained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemcial Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TBBPA, bisphenol A (BPA), 4-nitrophenol, hexafluorobisphenol A (BPAF), 4-nonylphenol (4-NP), bis(4-hydroxyphenyl) sulfone (BPS) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The other reagents and instruments for characterization were provided in detail in supplementary materials.

The target CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment with maleic acid as the carbon source based on previous report with minor modification (Ahmed et al., 2021). Firstly, 1.044 g maleic acid was mixed with 25 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution, and then diverted into a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, and later laid in an oven at 180 °C for 9 h. The autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature, and the solution was taken out and then filtered to obtain a clear yellow solution, which was treated with a dialysis bag (Molecular Weight Cutoff: 1000 Da) for 24 h to wipe out unreacted fine particles. The obtained clear solution was dried and prepared a 20 mg mL−1 CDs solution, and deposited in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of Fe3O4 NPs (Yuan et al., 2020) and Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 (Zhou et al., 2021a) were presented in the supplementary materials. The obtained nanomaterials were characterized with TEM and FTIR.

The synthesis of magnetic carbon dots was based on previous report with appropriate modifications (Wei et al., 2022). 0.6 g EDC and 0.9 g NHS were dispersed evenly into 30 mL solution of CDs (10 mg mL−1), and stirred for 15 min at ambient temperature. The gained solution was named as liquid A. 600 mg Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 was dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water which was named fluid B. Then liquid A and fluid B were mixed together and proceeded with mechanical stirring for 20 h. The obtained product was washed using water and ethanol alternately for 3 times, and afterthat dried overnight under vacuum at 50 °C. The obtained nanomaterials were characterized with TEM and FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modifying the glassy carbon electrode with magnetic carbon dots and CTAB
ce:section.ce:para: Before the electrode modification, the GCE was ground to the mirror with 0.3 μm alumina slurry, and further cleaned thoroughly with deionized water. Then, the GCE was ultrasonic with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 30 s and further completely dried at ambient temperature. The electrode modification was as follows: Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs was configured into 1 mg mL −1 homogeneous suspension with DMF as the solvent. 2 μL Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs suspension and 2 μL CTAB ethanol solution (0.2 mM) were modified onto GCE by droplet coating method and dried under ambient temperature (Yu et al., 2016).

In this experiment, a standard three-electrode system was adopted, in which modified GCE was employed as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode was employed as the reference electrode and Pt wire was employed as the auxiliary electrode. The different working electrodes were investigated by CV, scanning with 5 mM K3 [Fe(CN)]6/K4 [Fe(CN)]6 solution with 0.1 M KCl as the electroyte solution. The scanning potential ranged from −0.2V to +0.6V, and the scan rate was 50 mV s−1. In the optimization of the experimental conditions, DPV was used for analysis, and the measurement was carried out in a 0.2 M PBS solution containing 1 μM TBBPA. The DPV was also used to develop the method and analyze the real samples. The 0.2 M PBS solution containing diverse concentrations of TBBPA (pH 6.0) was prepared, and the three electrodes were put into the TBBPA solution for analysis (Wang et al., 2022). The deposition potential was 0 V, and the deposition time was 5 min, and the potential was in the range of 0.2 V–0.8 V. The real sample analysis was carried out under the same conditions.

The TEM image and fluorescent spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. S1 . The prepared CDs were evenly dispersed and almost spherical in shape. The particle size distribution diagram showed that the particle size of CDs was between 2.3 nm and 3.3 nm, and mainly around 2.5 nm (See Fig. S1A). In addition, the fluorescence properties of CDs was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S1B , the fluorescence intensity was in the range of 7000–8000. Both of these proved the successful synthesis of CDs. The TEM images of Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs and FTIR spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs were shown in Fig. 1 . As can be seen from Fig. 1, Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs particles are better dispersed than Fe3O4 particles. This may be due to the existence of repulsive forces such as electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance between the modified magnetic composites (Yuan et al., 2020). The average size of Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs were 14.13 nm. Compared with the average size of Fe3O4 NPs (9.08 nm), Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs particles were significantly larger, which proved that CDs were successfully modified onto Fe3O4 NPs.

The functional groups of Fe3O4 NPs, amino modified magnetic materials and magnetic CDs were characterized by FTIR, and results were exhibited in Fig. 1D. The typical absorption peaks at 2924 cm−1, 2848 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 were geared to the antisymmetric stretching vibration, symmetric stretching vibration and bending vibration of –CH2- groups, respectively. These specific peaks proved the existence of alkyl groups in the material. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 3434 cm−1 pertained to bending vibration and stretching vibration of –NH2 groups on Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs (Wu et al., 2020), respectively. The typical absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 also corresponded to the absorption peak of -CO-NH- groups on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs (Tong et al., 2021). The absorption at 590 cm−1 accounted for the vibration of Fe–O (Zhou et al., 2021b). The absorption at 1114 cm−1 was due to stretching vibration of Si–O on Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs (Wu et al., 2019), that proved that SiO2 was successfully coated on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs. The absorption at 1740 cm−1 was attributed to the typical stretching vibration of CO groups on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs (Liu et al., 2021), which were derived from the carbon source maleic acid, and aslo proved that CDs were successfully decorated onto Fe3O4 NPs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is an advanced analytical technique for surface analysis and provides the elemental and chemical composition. The XPS spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. S2 . Fig. S2A exhibited the typical peaks of O1s and C1s originated from the synthetized CDs. After correcting the binding energy with C1s, the binding energy peak of O1s was located at 532.3 eV (See Fig. S2B). Fig. S2C showed the Gaussian fitted C1s spectra of the three bands at 288.9, 286.6 and 284.8 eV for CO, C–C/C–O and CC, respectively. These observed peaks were consistent with the previously reported synthesis of CDs with maleic acid as a precursor.

CV is often utilized to investigate the modification performance of material-modified electrodes. The cyclic voltammetry behaviors of bare GCE, CDs/GCE, Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs/GCE and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE in the potential range of −0.2 V - +0.6 V were demonstrated in Fig. 2 A. The oxidation peak current (Ipa) of bare GCE was determined to be 39.8 μA. CDs/GCE displayed augmented Ipa of 41.29 μA. A higher Ipa was observed on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs/GCE, which can be attributed to large surface area and electrocatalytic activity of Fe3O4 NPs along with CDs. With the introduction of CTAB and positive charge of CTAB attracting [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- to electrode surface, Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE obtained the highest Ipa. This phenomenon manifested the best electron transfer rate on electrode surface at the moment and Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE provided the optimal electron transfer channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical responses to TBBPA of different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The DPV of different decorated electrodes soaked in PBS buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 6.0) with TBBPA spiked at 1 μM TBBPA for 5 min was shown in Fig. 2B. It was found that TBBPA underwent electrochemical oxidation reactions on different electrodes. There was a weak peak appeared from curve a at 0.616 V with current signal close to 0.15 μA. CTAB/GCE resulted in a small oxidation peak near 0.63 V (curve b), and the response current peak was approched to 0.32 μA, demonstrating good electrocatalytic activity of CTAB/GCE towards TBBPA molecule (Yu et al., 2016). The oxidation peak resulted from the loss of electron of hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring of TBBPA molecule, which made electron transfer to electrode and consequently the current response emerge. After being modified by Fe3O4 NPs-CTAB, the current peak value increased significantly (curve c), which was closed to 0.53 μA, this was mainly on account of splendid electrical conductivity and high active surface area of Fe3O4 NPs which signifincantly improved the electrochemical signal. The similarity of Fe3O4-CTAB/GCE (curve c) and Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2-CTAB/GCE (curve d) indicating SiO2–NH2 modification had no dramatic effect upon current signal. Further CDs modification achieved excellent enhanced electrochemical signal which probably abscribed to its electrocatalytic activity as curve e revealed. The synergistic effect between Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs and CTAB allowed the current signal to be amplified again, which made Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE have the best current response with a current peak value of approximate 1.24 μA being about 8.3 times higher than that of GCE.

The AC impedance spectra of bare GCE, CTAB/GCE, Fe3O4-CTAB/GCE, Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2-CTAB/GCE, Fe3O4@SiO2@ CDs-CTAB/GCE were shown in Fig. S4. Bare GCE had the largest semicircle, indicating its maximum impedance. The semicircle of CTAB/GCE, Fe3O4-CTAB/GCE and Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2-CTAB/GCE decreased successively. The semicircle of Fe3O4-CTAB/GCE and Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2-CTAB/GCE were close. Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE had the smallest semicircle, indicating that it had the lowest impedance, the highest electron transfer rate and the highest oxidation peak current, which was the same as the result in Fig. 2B.

For the sake of investigating electrochemical reaction mechanism of TBBPA on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE, CV scanning of Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE in PBS buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 6.0) containing 1 μM TBBPA in electrolyte was performed at different rates. Fig. S5A was the superimposed cyclic voltammograms with scan rates in the range of 50 mV s−1 to 500 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. S5B and S5C, while the scan rate varied from 50 mV s−1 to 500 mV s−1, the oxidation peak current (Ipa) of TBBPA had good linearities with the scan rate (ν) and its square root, and the linear regression equations were Ipa (μA) = 0.0022ν + 0.1981 (R2 = 0.9943) and Ipa (μA) = 0.0653ν1/2–0.2467 (R2 = 0.9933), respectively. This result manifested that Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE was probably controlled by the adsorption and diffusion process. In the next moment, the relationship between logν and logIpa was fitted (Fig. S5D), and a good linear equation was observed: logIpa = 0.6778 logν - 1.7385 (R2 = 0.9989). In general, the reaction on the surface of electrode is controlled by adsorption and diffusion processes with the slope of the equation between 0.5 and 1. Herein, the slope value was 0.6778 which demonstrated that the oxidation reaction of TBBPA on the surface of electrode was irreversible and impulsed by both adsorption and diffusion (Li et al., 2013b).
The meaning of α, n, ν, Eθ, R, T, F and kθ in above equation is in the supplementary materials.

The slope of the linear equation between Epa and the natural logarithm of scan rate (ln ν) is equal to the value of RT/αnF. From the above equation, the slope value was 0.0234 and so RT/αnF was equal to 0.0234. After calculation, the value of αn was 1.09. In the irreversible reaction process on the electrode, α is usually assumed to be 0.5, so that the electron transfer number (n) during the TBBPA reaction on the surface of the working electrode can be calculated as 2.19, which was approximately equal to 2. Therefore, it can be inferred that the oxidation reaction of TBBPA on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE was a two-electron electrochemical process.

To futher figure out the detection machanism of TBBPA on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE surface. Therefore, the electrochemical behavior of 1 μM TBBPA in buffer solutions with various pH values (pH 5–9) was investigated by CV, and the influence of supporting electrolyte pH on TBBPA response signal peak and oxidation peak potential was evaluated. Fig. S6A and S6B indicates that the oxidation peak current of TBBPA increased to the maximum value when the pH value of the solution was pH 6.0. This can be explained by two reasons. (1) According to the relation between pKa and pH: pKa = pH + log (undissociated concentration of analyte)/(ionized concentration), and the pKa of TBBPA is 8.5, so it can be concluded that the undissociated concentration of TBBPA was 316 times that of the dissociated ion. Therefore, the solute was mainly existed in undissociated status and non-dissociated TBBPA was easily diffused onto electrode surface. (2) The oxidation reaction of TBBPA on GCE surface was deprotonated, so it would block the electrode reaction under acid condition (Wang et al., 2019). Hence, pH 6.0 was utilized in the subsequent measurement process.

Fig. S6B showed the CV curves of Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE against TBBPA under different pH conditions. It can be clearly seen that the oxidation peak potential of TBBPA transferred to the negative direction with the increase of pH, which indicated that protons attended into the electrode reaction during the oxidating reaction of TBBPA. Fig. S6C depicts the linearity between TBBPA oxidation peak potential (Epa) and supporting electrolyte pH, and the equation was Epa(V) = −0.0553 pH + 0.9809 (R2 = 0.9547). On the basis of the Nernst equation, the slope value of this linear equation is approximately adjacent to the theoretical value of 0.0592V pH−1, which demonstrated that transfer of electrons in the reaction on the surface of the electrode was concomitant with equal number of protons (Wang et al., 2019). Therefore, it can be deduced that the oxidation reaction of TBBPA on Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE was a two-electron, two-proton electrochemical process. The process is as follows: The first step was the dissociation and dehydrogenation of the phenolic hydroxyl group on TBBPA to generate phenolic oxygen anions. and then the negatively charged phenol oxygen anions lost electrons to form free radicals. The reaction mechanism was demonstrated in Fig. 3 .

The concentration of CTAB can affect the thickness of the CTAB layer coated on the surface of the electrode, which will affect the enriched amount of TBBPA and further have impact on the detection performance of the electrode. In order to achieve an optimal concentration of CTAB, The experiments were carried out for optimization in the range of 0.1–0.6 mM. As shown in the Fig S7A, two variables were positively correlated from 0.1 mM to 0.2 mM. The Ipa of TBBPA increased step by step while the concentration of CTAB was augmented. The maximum Ipa of 0.83 μA was reached at the concentration of 0.2 mM. This may be that the increased concentration of CTAB resulted in the increase of deposited amount of CTAB which could capture more TBBPA due to the hydrophobic interaction between CTAB and TBBPA. Then more TBBPA will be oxidized, resulting in higher current. When the concentration of TBBPA exceeded 0.2 mM, the Ipa decreased dramatically along with CTAB changed from 0.2 mM to 0.6 mM. And the minimun Ipa of 0.31 μA was observed at the concentration of 0.6 mM. This may be owing to the excessive deposition of CTAB which increased CTAB layer thickness, further increased the electron-transfer resistance on the electrode surface and less electron produced by TBBPA oxidation can transfer to electrode surface. Consequently, the response signal was reduced due to its low conduction. Therefore, 0.2 mM was selected as the optimal modification concentration of CTAB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time and potential
ce:section.ce:para: For an adsorption process, the incubation time and potential are important parameters affecting the adsorption capacity of TBBPA. DPV method was employed to investigate the effects of incubation potential and time upon oxidation response signal of TBBPA. The impact of incubation potential on the anodic peak current of possible values was carried out throughout −0.4 to +0.4 V (Fig. S7B). The signal of sensor was highest at 0 V. when the incubation potential increased, the current signal decreased. Therefore, the incubation potential of 0 V was taken as the optimal condition for subsequent detection. Fig. S7C describes the effect of incubation time on the oxidation peak current of 1 μM TBBPA. When incubation time increased from 0 min to 5 min, the peak current value of TBBPA heightened as well. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that with the increase of incubation time, more TBBPA diffused and was adsorbed to the electrode, so that more TBBPA was oxidized, and then the oxidation peak current value gradually increased. Further prolonging incubation time, the peak current value of TBBPA was prone to be stable, which was ascribed to the saturation of the adsorption amount of TBBPA on the electrode surface and when the incubation time was extended, no more TBBPA would be diffused or adsorbed to the electrode. Consequently, for aquiring a higher response signal and improving the detection performance, the incubation time was set at 5 min in the subsequent detection.

With the optimized parameters, DPV method was employed to examine the response of Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB/GCE to a series of different concentrations of TBBPA. The decorated electrode was deposited in TBBPA solutions at different concentrations at a deposition potential of 0 V for 5 min, and further measured by DPV. The response curves of the sensor to different concentrations of TBBPA were exhibited in Fig. 4 A. By fitting the peak current value (Ipa) of TBBPA and the concentration (c) of TBBPA, the experimental results demonstrated that it was clear that the peak response signal of TBBPA exhibited an excellent linear increase trend along with the augmenter of TBBPA concentration in the scope of 1 nM–1000 nM (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation was Ipa (μA) = 0.0011c (nM) + 0.0384 (R2 = 0.997).The detection limit was calculated to 0.75 nM (LOD = 3σ/s), in which s is expressed as the slope of the standard curve and σ refers to the standard deviation of fifteen blank samples detected. However, in this study, on account of no oxidation peak current observed when blank sample was tested. Therefore, the standard deviation was required by determining the low concentration of TBBPA instead of the blank solution multiple times (Yao et al., 2019). The analytical features of proposed sensor for TBBPA was compared with the existing reports, and the results were shown in Table 1 . Compared to the reports (Zhou et al., 2016, 2018; Shen et al., 2018), the constructed sensor in present study provided a lower LOD and a wider linear range.

The repeatability of the designed sensor was inquired by measuring 500 nM TBBPA using six independent electrodes prepared under the same conditions and following the same procedure. The relative standard deviation was 4.8%, which is similar to previous report (Zhao et al., 2016, 2017a). This result described good repeatability of fabricated sensor. The intra-day precision was performed on the same electrode in buffer solution contaning TBBPA spiked with 500 nM six times in a day. Similarly, the inter-day precision was carried out on the same electrode in buffer solution contaning 500 nM TBBPA six times in six days. The intra-day and inter-day precisions (n = 6) were 3.5% and 4.0%, respectively. The prepared sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 10 days to study its stability. After 10 days, it was found that the current dropped to 90.3% of the initial current, which has lower stability compared to previous reported findings (Zhao et al., 2017b; Chen et al., 2014). But the results of this study were acceptable.

Considering that structurally similar phenolic substances may interfere with the determination of TBBPA, a selectivity investigation experiment was designed and performed. The concentrations of BPA, BPAF, BPS, 4-nonylphenol (4-NP) and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) were fixed on 5 μM and TBBPA was fixed on 500 nM. The results were depicted in Fig. 5 A. It can be seen that 4-NP and p-NP do not interfere with the detection of TBBPA in the potential range of 0.4–0.8V. And BPS had smaller oxidation peak current than TBBPA. The oxidation peak potentials of BPAF and BPA were 0.656 V and 0.64 V, respectively. They were quite different from the oxidation peak potential of TBBPA (0.592 V). The observed phenomenon made clear that the proposed sensor had good selectivity toward TBBPA (See Fig. 5A). Next, 5 μM of BPA, BPAF, BPS, 4-NP and p-NP were added into buffer solution contaning 500 nM TBBPA, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the five analogues had little effect on the determination of TBBPA. The peak current value of TBBPA did not change significantly with the presence of interferences. Therefore, it could be considered that all of them had no interference with the detection of TBBPA. From the above, it can be deduced that the sensor prepared with Fe3O4@SiO2@CDs-CTAB modified electrode has good selectivity and anti-interference performance.

For exploring the practicability of the prepared sensor, DPV detection of three kinds of beverages (Vitamin water, scream drink and genki forest) was carried out (Table 2 ). No peak oxidation current was observed, which indicated that TBBPA was not detected in any of the drinks. In order to validate the feasibility of the constructed sensor, spiked beverage samples were used. The spiked levels were 10 nM, 100 nM, and 300 nM, respectively. The achieved spiked recoveries were between 96.6% and 104.2%. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were in the range of 1.8%–4.6%.

In present study, a new low-cost, fast and sensitive electrochemical sensor for TBBPA was constructed. The raw materials for CDs are facile to obtain and Fe3O4 NPs are also easily prepared, and which reduced the cost of analysis. The magnetic carbon dot composites had a good enhancement effect on the oxidation of TBBPA, in addition, the hydrophobic effect of CTAB can enrich TBBPA, which further improves the electrochemical response. The prepared sensor earned a wide linear range of 1 nM–1000 nM and good detection limit of 0.75 nM. The experimental results demonstrated that the constructed sensor was fast and efficient and had high sensitivity and good stability. Furthermore, the sensor has also achieved satisfactory results for real samples (Vitamin water, scream drink and genki forest) with the spiked recoveries of real samples over the range of 96.6–104.2%. Besides, CDs was effectively utilized for measuring TBBPA, which expanded the application of carbon dots into the electrochemical field, and also provide new way for developing sensors for the detection of other pollutants.

Tongxu Qu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization.
